excluded_defs.name: Walk
excluded_defs.reason: read: public entry point that calls walk; no change needed here since the fix is entirely in the internal walk method

exploration_log.aha_moment: The `ancestors = append(ancestors, t)` pattern mutates the shared backing array when the slice has capacity. The WalkFunc receives the live slice so any stored reference sees later mutations. Creating a fresh copy for each walkFn call and for each recursive call eliminates the aliasing.
exploration_log.hindsight: Using `append(append([]*Route{}, ancestors...), t)` is idiomatic Go for a copy-then-append; it creates a new backing array each time.

alternatives: Copy only when passing to walkFn, keep mutate-and-restore for recursion
Use a separate stack instead of a slice
key_decisions.decision: Copy for walkFn call AND create fresh slice for each recursive call
key_decisions.reasoning: Both the walkFn and the recursive walk must receive independent slices; mutate-and-restore is insufficient because the WalkFunc may hold a reference to the passed slice

solve_notes: In the walk() method, changed the WalkFunc call to pass a copy of ancestors (make+copy). For subrouter recursion, replaced the mutate-and-restore append/truncate pattern with append(append([]*Route{}, ancestors...), t) which creates a fresh independent slice each time. This prevents any WalkFunc that stores the ancestors reference from seeing mutations.
task_text:
Fix Router.Walk sharing mutable ancestor slice across WalkFunc callbacks

In `mux.go`, the `walk` method reuses and mutates the `ancestors` slice
between iterations. It calls `ancestors = append(ancestors, t)` before
descending into a subrouter and truncates afterward, but the `WalkFunc`
receives the live slice. A callback that stores the `ancestors` reference
will see it mutated by later calls. Fix `walk` to pass a copy of the
ancestors slice to `WalkFunc` and when recursing into subrouters.

tier_difference_reasoning: The minimum set is just walk() which is the only edited function. The thrash_preventing set adds Walk (entry point, so an agent understands the initial slice state) and WalkFunc type (to understand the parameter contract). Without these, an agent would likely search for how Walk is called and what WalkFunc promises about slice ownership.
